reasoning: The TFR in South Korea has seen a decade-long persistent decline, dropping to 0.72 in 2023. In the first half of 2024, TFR continued to trend lower or flat compared to 2023, making it likely that 2024 will finalize around 0.68-0.70. For the 2025 TFR to exceed 0.72, it would require a significant reversal in trend. While there was a slight post-pandemic bump in marriages over 2023-2024 (which might yield some first-child births in 2025) and the denominator of reproductive-age women is shrinking, these are unlikely to be enough to overcome the massive structural barriers (housing costs, work culture) dragging down fertility. Furthermore, the suggested mechanism—a political and national crisis from the December 2024 martial law attempt—is highly likely to suppress rather than encourage short-term conceptions due to heightened uncertainty, and will only affect births in the final quarter of 2025 anyway. Given the strong downward momentum, a genuine bounce exceeding the 2023 baseline is very low probability.
